items.title: Customer churn with 500 engineered columns
items.scenario: A telecom team builds 500 behavioral features (counts, ratios, flags) from 8,000 labeled churn records. k-NN validation accuracy drops versus a 12-feature core set built by domain experts.
items.explanation: Many columns add noise dimensions; distances concentrate so neighbors share little churn signal. Canonical curse: sparsity plus distance concentration — not a bug in k-NN alone. Feature selection or PCA (see linked topics) recovers usable geometry.

items.title: Genomics with thousands of gene expressions
items.scenario: Microarray study: n ≈ 200 patients, d ≈ 20,000 gene expression levels, binary disease label. A baseline k-NN on raw genes overfits or ties random guessing; PCA to 50 components stabilizes cross-validation.
items.explanation: Classic n ≪ d regime: sample complexity is catastrophic for local methods in full gene space. Reducing d attacks sparsity; the curse explains why reduction is mandatory, not optional.

items.title: Image search with 784-pixel vectors
items.scenario: A museum digitization pilot flattens 28×28 grayscale patches to 784-D vectors and runs 1-NN retrieval. Queries return visually unrelated neighbors until PCA or learned embeddings shrink d.
items.explanation: Pixel space is high-dimensional; Euclidean distance on raw intensities poorly matches human similarity. Distance concentration makes many patches equidistant — reduction maps to a space where local neighborhoods align with semantics.

items.title: Fraud model with irrelevant GPS jitter features
items.scenario: An analyst appends 50 GPS coordinate noise dimensions to 10 transaction features. Tree model AUC unchanged; distance-based anomaly score becomes noisy because all points look equally far in the augmented space.
items.explanation: Irrelevant dimensions inflate d without signal — sparsity and distance concentration dilute the 10 useful columns. Demonstrates why feature discipline matters before any specific algorithm choice.

items.title: Dynamic programming on a 6-D state grid
items.scenario: Bellman's original motivation: discretize a robot state (x, y, θ, velocity, acceleration, fuel) into 10 bins per axis for value iteration. The state table needs 10⁶ ≈ 1M cells — infeasible to fill and update densely.
items.explanation: Historical origin of the phrase: grid size is exponential in state dimension (sample complexity for tabular methods). Modern ML faces the same combinatorics in feature space unless structure or function approximation is imposed.
